trajectory_summary: The call opened with a qualification friction spike (three-flat / tenant bills), recovered cleanly after a hold, and then expanded dramatically when Emery disclosed a $30M commercial property portfolio and $600K earmarked for a resort solar project. Meg booked the residential appointment but did not route the commercial signal — the residential sit is the audition for a much larger opportunity the closer will walk into without knowing it exists. The call shape is mixed: energy built in the rapport phase, but the commercial disclosure was absorbed without any routing action.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown. The surface outcome is a booked residential appointment (02:30 tomorrow). But the more consequential outcome question is whether the commercial lead signal was captured and routed. It was not. The closer will arrive for a residential consult without knowing Emery has $600K allocated for a Belize resort project and multiple Chicago apartment buildings in scope. The residential booking is real but incomplete as a lead outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill read-back accuracy and flip discipline
primary_coaching_focus.why: Meg misheard $181.80 as $1.80 — a data error that will corrupt the closer's proposal if it's in the CRM. Fix the record before 02:30 tomorrow. Then use the same moment to coach the flip: the bill was stated, acknowledged, and dropped. Both failures — the mishear and the missed flip — live at the same timestamp, making this the single most efficient coaching moment in the call.

key_moments.stewart_read: Emery states his bill clearly and precisely. Meg acknowledges and moves directly to credit score. The bill was captured and dropped — no flip, no emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The schema's capture-pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Meg executed 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' This is the primary missed flip opportunity in the call. The number was on the table; the emotional leverage was not taken.

Emery stated '$181.80.' Meg repeated back '$1.80 a month.' This is a concrete transcription/mishearing error. If this number enters the CRM or the closer's notes as $1.80, the proposal will be built on a figure two orders of magnitude below the actual bill. The closer walks in with a broken anchor.

key_moments.quote: so I'm a realtor, and I'm a investor, and I'm probably managing 30,000,000 in properties.
key_moments.stewart_read: Emery is not a residential solar prospect who happens to own a three-flat. He is a commercial real estate investor with $30M in properties, a 60-unit resort in Belize, multiple Chicago apartment buildings, and $600K allocated for solar. The residential unit is the entry point, not the deal. Meg absorbed this disclosure without routing it — she noted 'equipment details' in her notes but did not flag the commercial opportunity for a different team or specialist.

key_moments.quote: I got 600,000 to put solar on the resort right now. So I'm trying to figure out what's gonna be my best plan.
key_moments.stewart_read: Emery has capital allocated and is actively vendor-shopping for a commercial project. This is not a future possibility — it is a live buying signal with a stated budget. Meg's response ('For sure. For sure. So yeah. I mean, really, the first thing we need to do is get that usage.') pivots back to the residential bill collection without acknowledging the commercial signal at all. The schema's cross-sell routing logic applies: when a customer discloses a need beyond the residential scope, bridge and route.
